title: GPT-5.5 vs DeepSeek V4: model race accelerates — Apr 24
summary: OpenAI launched GPT-5.5, codenamed Spud, rolling it out to paid ChatGPT and Codex subscribers one week after Anthropic's latest release, while DeepSeek simultaneously dropped its open-source V4 model with 1.6 trillion parameters. OpenAI also launched ChatGPT for Clinicians, a free tool for medical professionals that benchmarks above human doctors on clinical tasks even when those doctors have unlimited time and internet access. Practitioners should watch the CI-Work benchmark, which found enterprise LLM agents leak sensitive data in up to 27 percent of retrievals — a direct risk for any financial or legal workflow deploying RAG-based agents today.

script:
Your Daily AI Press Review — April 24, 2026: Model Race.

OpenAI launched GPT-5.5, its most capable model yet, just one week after Anthropic's last release — and DeepSeek answered the same day with its open-source V4 at 1.6 trillion parameters. Meta announced 8,000 layoffs, about 10 percent of its workforce, as AI capital expenditure is set to rise at least 60 percent this year. Off the radar, DeepSeek's delayed V4 launch traces back to a serious training failure in mid-2025 and a migration from NVIDIA to Huawei Ascend chips — a supply-chain story Bloomberg hasn't fully told.

OpenAI released GPT-5.5, internally codenamed Spud, on Thursday — one week after Anthropic's latest model launch. Co-founder Greg Brockman described it as a new class of intelligence, faster and sharper per token than its predecessor, with stronger multi-step agentic capabilities and matching response speed in real-world use. It's live now in ChatGPT and Codex for paid subscribers. API access is being held back while OpenAI finalizes cybersecurity guardrails, with the company saying it will arrive very soon. The model improvements are strongest in coding and research tasks.

DeepSeek released V4 on Friday, its largest model ever, in two versions: V4-Pro at 1.6 trillion parameters and V4-Flash at 284 billion. The Hangzhou-based startup says V4 is competitive with leading closed-source models from OpenAI and Google DeepMind on efficiency benchmarks. The model uses a mixture-of-experts architecture and a sparse attention mechanism called DSA to reduce compute and memory requirements. DeepSeek says V4 is open-source. Notably, the release does not include multimodal generation — a capability that was widely anticipated but was deferred due to compute and funding constraints.

Anthropic is heading into a potential IPO that could value the company near 800 billion dollars, but it's hitting turbulence across nearly every part of its business. Revenue has tripled to about 30 billion dollars this year, driven largely by coding tools, yet the company faces compounding problems: perceived model quality declines in Opus 4.6, pricing pressure, capacity constraints, and a security incident involving unauthorized access to its Mythos model. OpenAI is actively courting frustrated Anthropic developers, pitching itself as the steadier alternative ahead of what could be dueling public offerings.

Meta told staff Thursday it plans to cut roughly 8,000 jobs — about 10 percent of the company. The move comes as capital expenditures are set to rise at least 60 percent this year compared with 2025, driven by Meta Superintelligence Labs and core infrastructure. Free cash flow is expected to fall about 83 percent year over year. The layoffs are framed as an AI efficiency push: Meta is also reportedly analyzing the work patterns of remaining employees to train agents that could automate those roles.

Google used its Cloud Next conference to consolidate all enterprise AI agent tooling under a single platform it's calling the Agentic Data Cloud. The pitch: building an agent, connecting it to live data, securing it, and governing it have historically required separate vendors and separate procurement decisions. Google Cloud's Andi Gutmans argued the company holds a structural advantage because no competitor currently combines cloud infrastructure, frontier models, and a data platform under one roof. The announcement also included A5X bare-metal instances running on NVIDIA Vera Rubin NVL72 rack-scale systems, targeting up to ten times lower inference costs.

Yann LeCun's AMI Labs has raised about 1 billion dollars in startup funding with just 12 employees — a ratio that signals extreme investor conviction in LeCun's thesis that large language models are not the path to general intelligence. LeCun, formerly chief AI scientist at Meta, is building toward what he calls world models: systems that reason about physical reality rather than predict tokens. The funding round makes AMI Labs one of the most capital-efficient bets in AI history on a per-employee basis.

Tencent released Hy3 Preview, its first flagship open-source model since former OpenAI researcher Yao Shunyu joined to lead foundational AI development. The Shenzhen-based company says Hy3 is on par with top Chinese models but still trails flagship products from OpenAI and Google DeepMind. The model is notably small for a flagship release. Separately, Xiaomi released MiMo-V2.5-Pro and MiMo-V2.5, two open-source agentic models the company says match frontier model benchmarks at significantly lower token cost.

Microsoft launched Agent Mode inside Word, Excel, and PowerPoint this week — what the company had previously called vibe working. It's a more powerful version of Copilot that can execute multi-step tasks autonomously inside Office documents. Separately, Microsoft is working with Anthropic through Project Glasswing to test Claude Mythos Preview using CTI-REALM, Microsoft's open-source cybersecurity benchmark, with the goal of identifying and mitigating vulnerabilities before they can be exploited at scale.

OpenAI launched a Trusted Access program giving Microsoft its most capable models specifically for cyber defense use cases. The program was triggered in part by the emergence of Anthropic's Mythos model, which can autonomously identify and exploit security vulnerabilities. OpenAI also released Privacy Filter, an open-source model designed to detect and redact personal data in text — a direct response to enterprise compliance requirements around data handling in LLM pipelines.

Sierra, the AI customer service agent startup founded by Bret Taylor, acquired Fragment, a YC-backed French startup. No financial terms were disclosed. The deal extends Sierra's European footprint and adds Fragment's capabilities to its enterprise agent platform. Separately, Alibaba struck its first external partnership for its Qwen consumer AI app, linking it with China Eastern Airlines to let users manage the full flight booking process — from search to check-in — inside a single natural-language chat interface.

On deployments. OpenAI launched ChatGPT for Clinicians, a free version of its chatbot for medical professionals. A new benchmark shows OpenAI's latest model outperforms human doctors on clinical tasks — even when those doctors have unlimited time and internet access. The product is rolling out to verified medical professionals in the US. It's the first major AI lab to offer a free, clinician-specific product at this capability level, and it puts direct pressure on specialized medical AI vendors like Nabla and Abridge.

PLDT became Southeast Asia's first telecom operator to deploy advanced agentic AI for enterprise information exchange. The Philippine carrier is using the system to accelerate data sharing between its business units and enterprise clients. The deployment positions PLDT as a regional reference case for telco-grade agentic AI, ahead of larger operators in Indonesia, Thailand, and Vietnam that are still in pilot phases.

Amazon Web Services published a detailed deployment guide for multimodal biological foundation models across drug discovery and clinical development. Trend Micro used Amazon Bedrock with Amazon Neptune and the Mem0 memory layer to build a persistent-context chatbot — called Trend's Companion — that retains company-specific knowledge across multiple customer interactions. The architecture lets the agent learn from prior sessions rather than starting cold each time, reducing repetitive queries and improving resolution rates.

Alibaba's Qwen app integration with China Eastern Airlines is live, covering the full booking workflow in natural language. Users can search flights, purchase tickets, select seats, and check in without leaving the chat interface. It's the first time Alibaba has opened Qwen's agentic capabilities to an external partner outside its own ecosystem, and it sets a template for travel, retail, and logistics verticals across China.

The US Pentagon has deployed roughly 100,000 AI agents on unclassified networks, built by workers using vibe-coding tools — meaning natural-language-driven development with minimal traditional programming. The scale is significant: 100,000 agents across a single organization is the largest known enterprise agentic deployment on record. The agents are limited to unclassified networks, and Anthropic has separately confirmed in a federal court filing that it has no kill switch or visibility once its models are deployed in classified settings.

A multi-agent AI system for guiding first-line immunotherapy decisions in non-small cell lung cancer — NSCLC — was presented at ASCO. The system uses four specialized agents: one that parses clinical notes, one that retrieves treatment protocols, one that cross-references contraindications, and one that generates a recommendation. Early clinical evaluation data shows the system reduces time-to-treatment-decision in complex cases, though full trial results are pending.

On tools. Google open-sourced DESIGN.md, the agent prompt behind its AI design tool Stitch. The format gives AI agents a structured, machine-readable blueprint for brand rules — typography, color, spacing, component logic — so agents can generate brand-consistent UI without human review of every output. Any team running an AI design agent can now drop a DESIGN.md file into their repo and have the agent follow brand guidelines automatically. It's the first open standard for brand governance in agentic design workflows.

Microsoft published LangChain.js for Beginners, a free open-source course of 8 chapters and over 70 runnable TypeScript examples. It covers building agents that reason, call tools, and query knowledge bases autonomously. The course is hosted on GitHub and requires only an API key to run. For JavaScript-native teams that have been locked out of Python-first agentic tooling, this is the most accessible on-ramp to production-grade agent development currently available.

OpenAI's latest model is now accessible through the Codex CLI's backend API endpoint — a semi-official channel that lets third-party tools like OpenCode and Pi hook into existing ChatGPT paid subscriptions without a separate API contract. OpenAI's Romain Huet confirmed in March that this is intentional: the company wants users to access Codex wherever they work, including JetBrains, Xcode, and Claude Code. For enterprise teams managing AI spend, this means one ChatGPT subscription can now power multiple coding environments simultaneously.

Stream2LLM is a new open-source serving system that overlaps context retrieval with LLM inference to cut time-to-first-token in RAG deployments. It introduces adaptive scheduling for two retrieval patterns — append mode for progressive context accumulation and update mode for iterative refinement — and uses longest-common-prefix matching to avoid redundant computation when inputs change mid-session. For teams running high-concurrency RAG pipelines, Stream2LLM addresses the core latency bottleneck that forces a choice between waiting for full context or proceeding with incomplete information.

Anthropic published a detailed postmortem on Claude Code quality degradation reported over the past two months. Three separate bugs in the Claude Code harness — not the model itself — caused material problems. The most impactful: a March 26 change to clear idle session memory triggered every turn instead of once, making Claude appear forgetful and repetitive in long sessions. The fix is now live. Teams running long-running Claude Code sessions should expect noticeably improved continuity and reduced repetition starting this week.

One signal to watch. Enterprise LLM agents are leaking sensitive data at rates that make current RAG architectures unsuitable for regulated industries without additional safeguards. The CI-Work benchmark, published this week on arXiv, tested frontier models on enterprise workflows across five information-flow directions and found privacy violation rates ranging from about 16 percent to nearly 51 percent, with sensitive data leakage reaching up to 27 percent. Critically, larger models and deeper reasoning did not reduce violations — they often increased them. The paper concludes that model-centric scaling cannot solve this problem; context-centric architectures are required.

The AI supply chain is becoming a geopolitical chokepoint. Hyperscalers are committing roughly 650 billion dollars to US AI infrastructure this year, according to Moody's David Pan, but that investment assumes stable access to Qatari helium, Israeli bromine, and LNG tankers transiting the Strait of Hormuz. The effective closure of the strait due to the Iran conflict is already squeezing industrial inputs that chip manufacturers depend on. Samsung and SK Hynix shares are rallying in Seoul as AI demand tightens memory supply — a signal that the hardware constraint is real and priced into Asian markets before it registers in US tech coverage.

Token spend inside enterprise engineering teams is growing faster than budgets can absorb. A survey of 15 tech companies published by The Pragmatic Engineer found rapid, unplanned growth in monthly token costs, with several teams reporting spend doubling quarter over quarter. AI vendors including Anthropic and OpenAI are simultaneously under capacity pressure, with Anthropic restricting third-party tools like OpenClaw to manage load. The combination of uncapped demand and constrained supply is pushing enterprises toward token governance — usage limits, cost attribution by team, and model tiering — as a new operational discipline.

Off the radar. DeepSeek's delayed V4 launch has a backstory that mainstream coverage is missing. According to Chinese tech publication 36Kr, citing multiple insiders, DeepSeek suffered a serious training failure in mid-2025 while migrating its training framework from NVIDIA to Huawei Ascend chips. Internal disagreements about training direction — including specific requirements from founder Liang Wenfeng that were difficult to execute — further delayed the release. The company has also opened an external fundraising window for the first time, driven by the need to train models beyond 1.6 trillion parameters and to retain talent after key researchers including R1 co-author Guo Daya were poached by ByteDance and Tencent.

Huawei is committing about 80 billion yuan — roughly 11.7 billion dollars — over the next five years to boost compute for training and testing its Qiankun ADS autonomous driving system. The investment is specifically for semi-autonomous vehicle training infrastructure, not general AI. CEO Jin Yuzhi confirmed the figure at a public briefing. This makes Huawei's autonomous driving compute budget larger than the total AI infrastructure spend of most European automakers, and it comes as Xpeng reported a 118 percent month-over-month increase in orders for its Ultra vehicle line after deploying its second-generation VLA driving model.

South Korea's creative and language workforce is experiencing measurable income decline from AI substitution, according to The Korea Times. Translators, copywriters, and illustrators report losing contracts to AI tools without consent or compensation frameworks in place. The Korea Creative Workers Union has begun documenting cases, but no national framework exists yet. This is the most concrete labor-market data point from any Asian economy on AI-driven income displacement in knowledge work — and it's arriving two to three years ahead of equivalent data from European or US labor surveys.

A Chinese startup called Ganjue Jieyu — operating under the brand Buzzy — raised over 20 million dollars led by Redpoint Ventures and is reporting about 20 million dollars in annual recurring revenue. Buzzy is a video editing agent that lets users make localized edits — background removal, lighting correction, product replacement, perspective changes — through natural language chat, without regenerating the entire video. The founding team includes former Apple AirPods system integration lead Ella Zhang. The product addresses a specific failure mode of current AI video editors: whole-frame regeneration when users want surgical edits.

On the research front. Apple researchers published ParaRNN at ICLR 2026 as an Oral paper — the highest recognition tier — presenting a framework that parallelizes the training of classical recurrent neural networks. The method achieves a 665-times speedup over sequential RNN training and enables, for the first time, a 7-billion-parameter RNN that matches transformer performance on language modeling benchmarks. RNNs generate tokens in constant time regardless of context length, unlike transformers whose compute grows quadratically with sequence length. This makes large RNNs directly relevant for on-device and low-latency inference deployments where transformer costs are prohibitive.

Google DeepMind published Decoupled DiLoCo, a distributed training architecture that breaks large model training into asynchronous, fault-isolated compute islands across geographically distant data centers. Standard distributed training requires about 198 gigabits per second of inter-datacenter bandwidth and stalls when any single chip slows down. Decoupled DiLoCo achieves 88 percent goodput — meaning 88 percent of theoretical maximum training throughput — even under high hardware failure rates, while requiring a fraction of that bandwidth. For organizations building or renting large training clusters, this directly reduces the cost and fragility of multi-datacenter training runs.

Researchers at Japan's National Institute for Materials Science, publishing in Nature, built an AI system that designs thermoelectric generators — devices that convert waste heat into electricity — about 10,000 times faster than conventional simulation-based methods. The system identified material combinations that conduct electricity while blocking heat transfer, a property that has historically required years of experimental iteration. Thermoelectric generators are used in industrial machinery, automotive exhaust systems, and wearable devices. The 10,000-times speed multiplier compresses a multi-year materials discovery cycle into days.

A benchmark called MSU-Bench — Musical Score Understanding Benchmark — tested more than 15 state-of-the-art language and vision-language models on 1,800 question-answer pairs drawn from works by Bach, Beethoven, Chopin, Debussy, and others. Every model tested showed pronounced gaps between text-based and visual score understanding, and none maintained consistent accuracy across difficulty levels. The result matters for any enterprise building multimodal document understanding systems: if frontier models fail on structured notation — a domain with clear rules — similar gaps likely exist in financial tables, engineering schematics, and legal forms.

This podcast has a daily production cost. If you enjoy it, support it — the link is on the podcast page. Thank you.
